# Streaming Content Cost Analysis: $1 Billion+ Per Percentage Point of US TV Consumption

### Plus, Implications for Marketers, As Growing Spending On Ad-Free/Ad-Light Services Creates New Challenges

![Madison and Wall's avatar](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!DD-4!,w_36,h_36,c_fill,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F1e3b65bd-1a69-4337-ab98-301dffcfd6b8_95x95.png)

#### Share this post

![Madison and Wall](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!SwBS!,w_36,h_36,c_fill,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep,g_auto/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F0782c308-1074-46d3-95c4-6c8e0f46d7d3_151x151.png)

*For anyone in London next Thursday March 22, I’ll be speaking at Video Week’s New Video Frontiers conference.  See* https://newvideofrontiers.com/ *for more details.  Hope to see you there!*

Thanks for reading Madison and Wall! Subscribe for free to receive new posts and support my work.

**Hypothesis: Content Spending Share Should = Audience Share**

In the past I have performed analyses which suggested a positive relationship between spending on content and consumer time with that content, which in turn has conveyed to me the importance of assessing content spending in order to assess the relative strength of different video-based services around the world.   I recognize that business models associated with streaming services may vary to some degree, that companies with streaming services may be at different stages of maturity, and that other factors may be at play as well, but in general I have presumed that the view that the cost to buy high-quality content should be roughly the same per unit of audience, regardless of which programmer or service wants to buy it.  In streaming specifically, as different services look to commission programs, I would expect that competition for programming ideas that would drive subscribers and/or reduce churn should lead to comparable prices for a given unit of content, defined in terms of the audience it is implicitly or explicitly expected to deliver.

These analyses have implications for revenue and profit growth for the services themselves, of course, but because streaming does not lend itself to high ad loads for most services and because many consumers will choose ad-free offerings, the growth of streaming probably results in lower reach of ad campaigns and substantially more expensive campaigns over time.

**New Data From Nielsen Plus New Company Data Drives The Analysis**

To arrive at an updated answer I thought I would look at Nielsen’s new “The Gauge” data which covers all uses of television sets for the period of February 2023, and which came out on Thursday.  The analysis here suggests that my expectations roughly hold.

To make comparisons between spending on programming and audiences, let’s start with numbers for which I have a high degree of confidence based on public disclosures of US-specific data.  Here we can look at spending by the two biggest US-only services, Disney’s Hulu and Comcast’s Peacock.

Per Disney’s SEC filings, Hulu’s calendar year 2022 spending on all programming amounted to $7.8 billion.   Of course, this includes the vMVPD Hulu Live which we want to exclude from the analysis to match viewing of the AVOD/SVOD service that’s tracked in the Gauge.   Assuming programming costs for the 4.1-4.5 million subscribers on its vMVPD service last year was around $65 per month I can estimate programming costs for the SVOD service alone amounted to $4.5 billion, which resulted in 3.3% of all TV viewing during February 2023.  2022 viewing for the SVOD service appears roughly similar, so on that basis we could say that costs amount to around $1.4 billion per percentage point of viewing.   Meanwhile, Comcast said at the beginning of 2022 that its content spend for Peacock would amount to $3 billion in 2022.  While viewing was not broken out for Peacock on the Gauge until December 2022, the service saw 1.0% of all TV use in February 2023 and also for the prior two months.

For other companies, this analysis requires more guesswork.  We know that Netflix accounted for 7.3% of total TV viewing in February 2023 per Nielsen, and figures for 2022 were similar on average.  We know that global content spending (Netflix’ amortized content expense) was $14 billion during 2022.  While the company doesn’t disclose country-specific content expenses, if we assume that the percentage of content costs allocatable to the US are slightly higher than the percentage of subscribers who are in the US (given the higher costs of US-based content and historical data conveying as much), I could roughly estimate costs of around $6 billion.    That’s around $800 million per percentage point of viewing – much lower than the others, but perhaps unsurprising given Netflix’ scaled penetration and relatively longer history in many consumer homes.

Meanwhile, at Amazon, US streaming viewing on Prime Video amounted to 3.0% of all TV consumption in February 2023 and 2.7% on average during 2022.   We know that global spending on video (including originals, live sports and licensed third-party content) amounted to $7 billion.   However, once again we don’t know how much of this cost should be allocated to the US. If we assume it’s half, or $3.5 billion, that’s around $1.2 billion per percentage point of viewing if correct.

Finally, among those companies for whom we have useable data, Disney+ incurred $5.8 billion in global content spending during 2022, and again if we presume half, or $2.9 billion could be allocated to the US, I would compare this figure to Disney+’s 1.8% of total TV viewing during February 2023, which was slightly below the average of around 1.9% for all of 2022.   This equates to around $1.5 billion per percentage point of viewing.

Other services such as HBO Max are harder to apply this analysis to directly, as the linear HBO service is still substantial with around 30 million subscribers who probably account for viewing that could be equal to or greater than the amount of viewing associated with the streaming product (which, incidentally, represented 1.3% of TV viewing in Feb. 2023).   Moreover, programming costs are not broken out by the company on a country or product basis.  Similarly, there is no data provided on viewing for Paramount+, let alone Showtime’s streaming product in the Gauge data, so a direct analysis of that service is not possible either.

Overall, for those services where we can make reasonable estimates, I calculate that content on streaming services costs roughly $1.2 billion per percentage point of audience.   There are outliers such as Peacock, which is a newly scaling service and Netflix, which is much more mature, but the average appears to be broadly reflective of the business in the United States.

**Total Content Spending By Distributors (Pay TV + Streaming) = ~$87 Billion in 2022**

Building on this data, I can make some bigger industry-level observations.  To look at the total amount of spending on professional streaming content, adding all streaming services up from this data and excluding YouTube’s non vMVPD product and its vMVPD product alike, 26% of TV consumption was associated with conventional streaming services during February 2023.   On a comparable basis for the full year 2022, the equivalent figure was probably closer to 22%.   Using the average cost figure of $1.2 billion per percentage point of viewing, this would equate to $27 billion of content spending for streaming services.     Based on an analysis of programming expenses from traditional pay TV services, I can estimate that there was around $60 billion in spending on programming from conventional pay TV services, which occurred on approximately 60% of TV viewing covering both broadcast stations and networks and cable networks as well (recall that while broadcasting programming is technically available for free for consumers who use an antenna, most access it through the MVPDs who in turn pay broadcasters retransmission consent fees). If that’s correct, viewing of linear programming costs around $1 billion per percentage point of TV consumption.

**Continued Spending Growth On Streaming Services = More Viewing on Streaming and More Challenges For Advertisers**

Looking to some implications around the business in the future, I note that despite rhetoric around controlling costs, spending on streaming services is likely to go up while spending on linear services probably holds stable or declines.   Increased spending on streaming is likely so long as there are large players with streaming services who lack legacy media businesses (Netflix, Amazon and Apple) who have reasons to continue increasing their investments, even if incrementally. For them, their increases in spending will likely contribute to share gains taken from anyone who cuts costs if everything else were held equal.

At the same time, some of the services have been explicit in anticipating spending increases, as we have seen with Peacock (Comcast has said they will increase spending on Peacock towards $5 billion in 2024 vs. $3 billion last year) and Paramount (whose spending should essentially double on Paramount+ from $3 billion to $6 billion at a global level).   It’s true that Warner Bros. Discovery might try to curtail growth in spending, but their subscriber goals suggest that they’ll have to increase it regardless.   Lastly, Disney has backed off of its subscriber goals in favor of a profitability focus, but similarly it seems unlikely that the company will be able to avoid spending more on its programming unless it wants to go backwards in size.

It's important to keep in mind that all of this has consequences for marketers so long as the bulk of streaming viewing is not likely to be ad-supported.  This means that it will be increasingly difficult to cost effectively manage campaigns against reach-based goals.  While connected TV-based inventory may help find those incremental points of reach in a cost effective way, most marketers who care about reach and frequency of video-based ad campaigns will either need to include YouTube (or TikTok, so long as it is around) in their plans or otherwise find other metrics or media to focus on.

Thanks for reading Madison and Wall! Subscribe for free to receive new posts and support my work.

# The Complex Economics of Streaming Services

## More users = higher prices?

![Muhammad Dimas Prasetyo](https://miro.medium.com/v2/resize:fill:64:64/1*jvO0etW3xemDV8TjmOiXkQ.jpeg)

--

Listen

Share

For all of you who are wondering: *Is it true that in a streaming service, as more people use it, more resources are needed to accommodate the users?* This means that more money is required, and as a result, the subscription price will keep increasing as the number of users grows.

**Not necessarily**. While it’s true that more users require more resources (like servers, bandwidth, and customer support), streaming services operate on a model with [economies of scale](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Economies_of_scale). This means that as the number of subscribers increases, the cost per user **can actually decrease**.

*— But the reality is far more nuanced.*

Some streaming services have raised prices over time (e.g., Netflix, Hulu), but this is often due to a combination of factors, including rising content production costs, inflation, and the need to fund new shows and movies — not just user growth. Other services, like Spotify, have kept prices relatively stable despite growing user bases, relying on economies of scale and diversification (e.g., podcasts, ad-supported plans).

![]()

Let’s dive into the economics of streaming to understand how these platforms manage costs, compete for users, and decide when (or if) to raise prices.

## The Cost of Streaming: What Drives the Expenses?

Running a streaming service isn’t cheap. The major costs can be broken down into three categories:

## Economies of Scale: How User Growth Can Reduce Costs

While more users mean higher overall costs, they also bring benefits. This is where **economies of scale** come into play. As a streaming service grows, the cost per user often decreases. Here’s why:

In short, while scaling up requires investment, it also creates opportunities for cost savings.

## Competition and Pricing: The Balancing Act

The streaming market is fiercely competitive, with dozens of platforms vying for viewers’ attention. This competition plays a significant role in pricing strategies:

## Revenue Diversification: Beyond Subscriptions

To offset costs and reduce reliance on subscription fees, streaming platforms have diversified their revenue streams:

## Case Studies: Price Increases and Their Impact

Let’s look at two examples of how streaming services have handled price increases:

These examples show that while price increases can be risky, they are often necessary to fund content investments and maintain profitability.

## The Future of Streaming Economics

The streaming industry is still evolving, and several trends could shape its future:

## Conclusion: A Balancing Act

So, does more usage lead to higher prices? The answer is both yes and no. While more users do increase costs, economies of scale and revenue diversification can help offset these expenses. Competition also plays a crucial role, as platforms must balance the need to invest in content and technology with the risk of losing subscribers to rivals.

Ultimately, the economics of streaming are a delicate balancing act. As the industry continues to evolve, platforms will need to innovate and adapt to stay competitive — without pricing themselves out of the market. For consumers, this means more choices and better content, but also the possibility of higher prices in the long run.

What’s clear is that the streaming revolution is far from over, and its economic complexities will continue to shape the way we watch and pay for entertainment.

### Cass Almendral
#### Hamptons Deal-Flow | 35K-Circulation Mag • 1.2K HNW Event Guests • $3M Flip Exits
Published Sep 5, 2024
[+ Follow](https://www.linkedin.com/signup/cold-join?session\_redirect=%2Fpulse%2Fanalysis-cost-structures-revenue-models-media-content-cass-almendral-smzqe&trk=article-ssr-frontend-pulse\_publisher-author-card)
### Introduction
The media landscape is a complex ecosystem where content creation is driven by various cost structures and revenue models. From independent creators to traditional studios, the dynamics of producing and monetizing content have been fundamentally altered by the rise of digital platforms. This analysis explores the cost structures and revenue models across different types of media content creation, examining how digital platforms have impacted both independent creators and traditional studios. The report provides a detailed understanding of the current state of the media business, offering insights into its challenges, opportunities, and future directions.
### Cost Structures in Media Content Creation
### 1. Independent Creators
Independent creators, such as YouTubers, podcasters, and influencers, operate under a relatively low-cost structure compared to traditional media. The key components of their cost structure include:
\* Equipment: Initial investment in equipment such as cameras, microphones, and editing software can range from a few hundred to several thousand dollars. However, many creators start with minimal equipment and upgrade as their revenue grows.
\* Production: Independent creators often handle production themselves, reducing costs associated with hiring a crew. This DIY approach allows them to keep overhead low, but it can limit the scale and production quality of their content.
\* Marketing and Distribution: Digital platforms like YouTube, Instagram, and TikTok provide free or low-cost distribution channels, significantly reducing the cost of reaching an audience. However, creators may invest in social media advertising or collaborations to grow their audience.
\* Time and Labor: For many independent creators, time is the most significant cost. Creating, editing, and promoting content requires substantial time investment, often with no immediate financial return.
### 2. Traditional Studios
Traditional studios, including those producing films, television shows, and high-budget digital content, operate under a significantly higher cost structure:
\* Development: Pre-production costs include scriptwriting, securing rights, casting, and location scouting. These costs can be substantial, especially for large-scale projects.
\* Production: The actual filming or creation of content involves costs related to hiring a crew, actors, special effects, set design, and equipment. These costs can run into millions of dollars, particularly for blockbuster films or high-budget TV shows.
\* Post-Production: Editing, sound design, visual effects, and other post-production processes are labor-intensive and expensive. High-quality post-production is crucial for traditional media to meet audience expectations.
\* Marketing and Distribution: Traditional studios invest heavily in marketing, often spending as much on promoting a film or TV show as they do on production. Distribution costs include securing theater space, broadcasting rights, and digital distribution platforms.
\* Overhead and Administrative Costs: Traditional studios have significant overhead costs, including office space, staff salaries, and corporate expenses, which add to the overall cost structure.
### Revenue Models in Media Content Creation
### 1. Independent Creators
Independent creators typically rely on a mix of revenue streams:
\* Ad Revenue: Platforms like YouTube offer ad revenue sharing through programs like Google AdSense. Creators earn money based on the number of views and clicks their content generates.
\* Sponsorships and Brand Deals: As creators build their audiences, they attract sponsorships from brands looking to reach their followers. These deals can be lucrative, particularly for creators with large, engaged audiences.
\* Merchandising: Many creators monetize their brand through merchandise sales, offering products like clothing, accessories, and digital goods.
\* Crowdfunding and Donations: Platforms like Patreon allow creators to receive direct support from their fans. Crowdfunding has become a significant revenue stream for creators who offer exclusive content or perks to supporters.
\* Paid Subscriptions: Some platforms, like YouTube and Twitch, offer paid subscription models where fans pay for premium content, ad-free experiences, or direct interaction with the creator.
### 2. Traditional Studios
Traditional studios rely on more established, yet evolving, revenue models:
\* Box Office and Ticket Sales: For films, box office revenue remains a primary income source. However, this model has been challenged by the rise of streaming platforms.
\* Broadcasting and Syndication Rights: TV shows generate revenue through licensing deals with networks and streaming platforms. Syndication of successful shows can provide long-term revenue streams.
\* Home Entertainment Sales: Revenue from DVD, Blu-ray, and digital downloads remains relevant, though it has declined with the growth of streaming services.
\* Subscription Services: As studios launch their streaming platforms (e.g., Disney+, HBO Max), subscription fees have become a critical revenue source, allowing them to bypass traditional distribution channels.
\* Product Placement and Sponsorships: Incorporating brands into content through product placement and sponsorship deals provides an additional revenue stream, particularly for films and TV shows with high viewership.
\* Merchandising and Licensing: Successful franchises generate significant revenue through merchandising and licensing deals, particularly in the case of films and TV shows with strong brand identities.
### Impact of Digital Platforms on Independent Creators and Traditional Studios

[![Image 6: Content Creation vs Production: Collaboration or Competition?](https://www.linkedin.com/pulse/analysis-cost-structures-revenue-models-media-content-cass-almendral-smzqe) Content Creation vs Production: Collaboration or… Chrome Productions 1 year ago](https://www.linkedin.com/pulse/content-creation-vs-production-collaboration-competition-8an0e)
### 1. Empowerment of Independent Creators
Digital platforms have empowered independent creators by providing accessible and scalable distribution channels. These platforms enable creators to reach global audiences without the need for significant upfront investment or gatekeepers. This democratization of content creation has led to a surge in diverse and niche content that traditional media might not have supported.
However, the revenue potential for independent creators is highly variable. While some have found immense success, many struggle to monetize their content effectively, given the competitive nature of digital platforms. The reliance on platform algorithms, which can change unpredictably, adds another layer of uncertainty for creators seeking to build sustainable income.
### 2. Disruption of Traditional Studios
Digital platforms have significantly disrupted traditional studios by shifting consumer preferences toward on-demand content and streaming services. The decline of physical media sales and box office revenues has forced studios to adapt by investing in their streaming services and exploring new revenue models.
This shift has led to increased competition among studios to produce high-quality, original content that can attract and retain subscribers. Traditional studios have had to rethink their distribution strategies, often bypassing theaters in favor of direct-to-consumer streaming releases. The rise of digital platforms has also eroded the exclusivity of content, with piracy and content sharing posing ongoing challenges.
### 3. Convergence and Collaboration
Despite the challenges, there is a growing trend of convergence and collaboration between independent creators and traditional studios. Studios increasingly recognize the value of social media influencers and digital creators in reaching younger audiences. Collaborations between traditional media companies and independent creators have led to new content formats and marketing strategies that leverage the strengths of both parties.
For example, traditional studios may partner with popular YouTubers to create branded content or tap into the creator's audience for promotional purposes. Conversely, independent creators benefit from the resources and expertise of traditional studios, allowing them to scale their content production and reach.
### Challenges, Opportunities, and Future Directions
### Challenges
\* Monetization: Both independent creators and traditional studios face challenges in monetizing content effectively. For creators, platform dependency and fluctuating ad revenues pose risks, while studios grapple with the high costs of producing and marketing content in a fragmented media landscape.
\* Content Saturation: The explosion of content on digital platforms has led to saturation, making it difficult for creators and studios to stand out. This environment increases the pressure to produce consistently high-quality, engaging content.
\* Piracy and Intellectual Property: The ease of content distribution online has made piracy a significant issue, particularly for traditional studios. Protecting intellectual property rights in the digital age remains a complex challenge.
### Opportunities
\* Direct-to-Consumer Models: The rise of direct-to-consumer (DTC) models offers significant opportunities for both independent creators and traditional studios. Subscription services, crowdfunding, and direct sales allow for more control over revenue streams and audience relationships.
\* Niche Markets: Digital platforms have enabled the growth of niche markets, where content tailored to specific audiences can thrive. Independent creators and studios that cater to these markets can build loyal, engaged communities.
\* Technology Integration: The integration of new technologies, such as artificial intelligence, virtual reality, and blockchain, presents opportunities for innovation in content creation, distribution, and monetization. These technologies can enhance user experiences and create new revenue streams.
### Future Directions
\* Hybrid Revenue Models: The future of media content creation will likely involve hybrid revenue models that combine advertising, subscriptions, sponsorships, and direct sales. Flexibility in revenue generation will be crucial for adapting to changing consumer preferences and market conditions.
\* Sustainability and Ethical Practices: As consumers become more conscious of sustainability and ethics, media creators and studios will need to adopt practices that align with these values. Transparency, responsible content creation, and sustainable production methods will become increasingly important.
\* Global Expansion: The global nature of digital platforms allows for content creators and studios to reach international audiences. Expanding into emerging markets presents significant growth opportunities, but also requires a deep understanding of diverse cultural preferences and consumption habits.
### Conclusion
The media content creation landscape is in a state of flux, driven by evolving cost structures, revenue models, and the impact of digital platforms. Independent creators and traditional studios face both challenges and opportunities as they navigate this dynamic environment. By embracing new technologies, exploring hybrid revenue models, and adapting to changing consumer demands, content creators across the spectrum can position themselves for success in the future media ecosystem. The key to thriving in this landscape will be the ability to innovate, collaborate, and maintain a focus on quality and audience engagement.

enriched_points.expanded_content: Producing original content involves substantial investments, averaging $3 million per episode for high-quality series, significantly impacting platforms' overall budgets.
Platforms incur substantial costs for licensing existing content, essential for attracting subscribers and ensuring a diverse library, resulting in competitive bidding among services.
Operational costs include server maintenance, bandwidth, and customer support, which can escalate with user growth but benefit from economies of scale.
Platforms invest heavily in marketing to attract and retain subscribers, often spending as much on marketing as on content production, reflecting fierce competition.
